solution.p: We will factor r^{2}+15r+56 where a=1, b=15, and c=56.
Mental Method: When we factor a polynomial in the form of x^2+bx+c, x^2+bxy+cy^2, or x^2y^2+bxy+c we can use mental math to factor. We need to find two numbers whose product is c, and whose sum is b.
For r^{2}+15r+56, since the product of 8 and 7 is 56, and the sum of 8 and 7 is 15, the factorization is \begin{aligned}[t]r^{2}+15r+56\amp =\left(r+8\right)\mathopen{}\left(r+7\right)\end{aligned}
AC Method: In this method, we first calculate the product of a and c in ax^2+bx+c. For this problem, we have (1) \cdot (56) = 56.

The number 56 can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways:  egin{array}{lll}1(56)\amp -1(-56)\amp 2(28)\amp -2(-28)
ewline4(14)\amp -4(-14)\amp 7(8)\amp -7(-8)
ewline\end{array} Looking at the corresponding sums, we want a sum of 15, and this sum happens with 7+8=15 So we write  egin{aligned}[t]r^{2}+15r+56\amp =r^{2}+7r+8r+56
ewline \amp =r\mathopen{}\left(r+7ight)+8\mathopen{}\left(r+7ight)
ewline \amp =\left(r+8ight)\mathopen{}\left(r+7ight)\end{aligned}Generic Rectangles Method: First, we set up generic rectangles by putting the first term and third term in r^{2}+15r+56 into the top left and bottom right rectangles:
The first term's coefficient, 1, can be factored into the product of two numbers (where the first factor is positive) in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(1)\amp \end{array}

The third term, 56, can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(56)\amp -1(-56)\amp 2(28)\amp -2(-28)\newline4(14)\amp -4(-14)\amp 7(8)\amp -7(-8)\newline\end{array}

We put each pair into the corresponding places next to those generic rectangles, and try to match the area of those rectangles with r^{2}+15r+56 by guess-and-check. The following generic rectangles show the solution:
Dimensions of those generic rectangles in the diagram give us the solution: \begin{aligned}[t]r^{2}+15r+56\amp =\left(r+8\right)\mathopen{}\left(r+7\right)\end{aligned}
